coredata.dc:title: Enhancing photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots by thermal annealing of the graphite precursor
coredata.dc:creator: Jovanović, Svetlana P.
Marković, Zoran M.
Syrgiannis, Zois
Dramićanin, Miroslav D.
Arcudi, Francesca
Parola, Valeria La
Budimir, Milica D.
Marković, Biljana M. Todorović
coredata.dc:description: Different approaches for enhancement of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) photoluminescence have been developed lately. We report modified electrochemical procedure for GQDs synthesis. Graphitic rods were thermal annealing at 1700°C in vacuum and used for electrochemical quantum dots synthesis. We found that the modified GQDs (GQDs-1700) consist of two while in the structure of pristine (p-GQDs) three graphene layers are measured. The main structural difference between GQDs is the increased structural disorder, caused by a higher amount of the oxygen containing functional groups in the structure of GQDs-1700. The photoluminescence of GQDs-1700 is 4 times more intense, red shifted and with doubled Quantum Yield compared to p-GQDs. In addition, the changes in electrochemical behavior of GQDs are observed: the higher capacitance was noticed if GQDs posses more reducible functional groups, such as peroxyl, aldehyde and epoxy groups while increase in keto functional groups leads to the lower capacitance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Nanostructures
B. Luminescence
C. Atomic force microscopy
C. Electrochemical measurements

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural properties and photoluminescence of GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Republic of Serbia
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministarstvo Prosvete, Nauke i Tehnološkog Razvoja
Vedecká Grantová Agentúra MŠVVaŠ SR a SAV

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge Prof. Maurizio Prato for valuable comments and discussion, Prof. Ivanka Holclajtner-Antunović for Raman measurements and Dr. Nadica Abazović for diffusive reflectance measurements. This research was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Republic of Serbia (project no. 172003) and project of bilateral collaboration between Serbia and Slovakia, 2017–2018 (project no. SK-SRB-2016-0038). Also, this work was supported by the SASPRO Programme, project no. 1237/02/02-b. The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union's Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427. Research has been further co-funded by the Slovak Academy of Sciences and by the VEGA project (2/0093/16).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of as-received electrode (black curve) and the annealed electrode (red curve). Raman spectra have been normalized and all spectra are vertically displaced for clarity. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

TEM micrographs of p-GQDs (a) and GQDs-1700 (b). Top view AFM image of p-GQDs (c, in the insert: height profile along with the yellow line), height (d) and diameter distributions (e). AFM image of GQDs-1700 (f, in the insert: height profile along with the yellow line), the histograms of height and diameter distribution for GQDs-1700 (g and h, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis spectra of p-GQDs (black curve) and GQDs-1700 (red curve), respectively. (b) XPS survey of p-GQDs (black line) and GQDs-1700 (red line). (c, e) deconvoluted C1s and O 1s XPS spectra of pristine GQDs and (d, f) GQDs-1700, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) FT-IR spectra of p-GQDs (curve 1) and GQDs-1700 (curve 2); (b) Raman spectra of p-GQDs (curve 1) and GQDs-1700 (curve-2), respectively.

(a) PL spectra and (b) PL decay curves of p-GQDs (curve 1) and GQDs-1700 (curve 2) measured at ambient temperature with the pulsed laser excitation at 328nm. (c) PL spectra of p-GQDs and (d) GQDs-1700 excited at different wavelengths. The concentrations of all GQD dispersions were 1mg/ml.
PL spectra of p-GQDs (a) and GQDs-1700 (b) in aqueous solutions at pH between 1, 7 and 13, laser excitation of 341nm. The concentrations of all GQD dispersions were 1mg/ml.
CV curves of p-GQDs and GQDs-1700 during fist 7 cycles (a and b, respectively) and at different scan rates, from 25 to 750mVs−1 (c and d, respectively).

(a) CV curves of p-GQDs (black curve) and GQDs-1700 (red curve) at scan rate of 25mVs−1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Illustration of GQDs formation during electrochemical oxidation from as-received and annealed electrodes.
XPS data for p-GQDs and GQDs-1700.

head.ce:title: Enhancing photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots by thermal annealing of the graphite precursor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences, University of Belgrade, Mike Alasa 12-14, 11001 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Institute Slovak Academy of Sciences, Dúbravská cesta 9, 845 41 Bratislava, Slovak Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Institute Slovak Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, INSTM, Center of Excellence for Nanostructured Materials (CENMAT), University of Trieste, via L. Giorgieri 1, 34127 Trieste, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences
Center of Excellence for Nanostructured Materials (CENMAT)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Istituto per lo Studio dei Materiali Nanostrutturati (ISMN-CNR), via Ugo La Malfa 153, I-90146 Palermo, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Istituto per lo Studio dei Materiali Nanostrutturati (ISMN-CNR)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering, University of Belgrade, Bulevar kralja Aleksandra 73, 11120 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Different approaches for enhancement of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) photoluminescence have been developed lately. We report modified electrochemical procedure for GQDs synthesis. Graphitic rods were thermal annealing at 1700°C in vacuum and used for electrochemical quantum dots synthesis. We found that the modified GQDs (GQDs-1700) consist of two while in the structure of pristine (p-GQDs) three graphene layers are measured. The main structural difference between GQDs is the increased structural disorder, caused by a higher amount of the oxygen containing functional groups in the structure of GQDs-1700. The photoluminescence of GQDs-1700 is 4 times more intense, red shifted and with doubled Quantum Yield compared to p-GQDs. In addition, the changes in electrochemical behavior of GQDs are observed: the higher capacitance was noticed if GQDs posses more reducible functional groups, such as peroxyl, aldehyde and epoxy groups while increase in keto functional groups leads to the lower capacitance.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been great interest in recently discovered graphene 0D carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, with lateral size less than 100nm and graphene lattices inside their structure [1–3]. Regardless of their nano-size, they have unique properties and possible applications in photocatalysis, bioimaging, ion detection, electrochemical sensing, in optoelectronic device as well as precursors in the synthesis of graphene [4–10]. Although Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are considered as carbon dots they display several differences. The main one is structural: carbon dots are either amorphous or crystalline while GQDs have domains of π-conjugated sp2 hybridized C atoms in the structure [3,5,11].

Even though many different synthetic procedures for GQD preparation have been developed, they all can be classified into two main groups: top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down methods are based on cutting of graphene-like materials with electron beam lithography [1,12], strong acids [13,14], electrochemical oxidation [3], exfoliation and disintegrating [15] as well as microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis [16]. Bottom-up methods include solution chemistry [17], cyclodehydrogenation of polyphenylene precursors [18], carbonizing of a certain organic molecules [19,20], chemical vapor deposition [21], fragmentation of suitable precursors, etc. [22].

Differences in GQD structure result in changes of photoluminescence [23], biological behavior [24,25] and electrochemical properties [26,27]. Thus GQDs have been proposed as electrode materials for high performance supercapacitors [28], as impending electrocatalyst for the development of efficient electrochemical sensors [27], while with sufficient number of acidic oxygen bearing groups GQDs can serve as an efficient electrolyte for supercapacitors [29].

In this work, we investigated a new approach for structural modification of GQDs introducing a pre-treatment of graphite rods in previously described electrochemical procedures for GQD synthesis. Graphitic rods for electrochemical synthesis were modified by thermal annealing at 1700°C in vacuum. Different characterization techniques were employed to study structural, morphological, optical and electrochemical properties. The influence of graphite pre-treatment on the photoluminescence, lifetime and quantum yield is investigated. By exploring the electrochemical properties of GQD prepared from both non-treated and pre-treatment of graphite, better capacitance behavior was noticed if GQDs posses more reducible functional groups, such as peroxyl, aldehyde and epoxy groups.

The spectroscopic graphite electrodes were bought from Ringsdorff Werke GmbH (Bonn, Germany), 99.999% purity (3.05mm in diameter). The electrodes were rinsed thoroughly with MiliQ water, ethanol (98%) and allowed to dry. Same procedure was used for annealed electrodes.

GQDs are prepared using previously described electrochemical procedure [30]. Graphite rods are used as both anode and cathode. For synthesis of GQD-1700 sample, graphite electrodes were annealed at 1700°C in vacuum for 3h (TorVac system, United Kingdom). As electrolyte, we used 3% NaOH dispersion in ethanol. The current intensity was set at 20mA. After 8h, the electrolyte was collected and neutralized with HCl. After that, formed NaCl was flocculated and separated from solution of GQD in ethanol by filtration. Ethanol solution was evaporated and the residual solid was collected. This procedure was repeated twice. Then, ethanol evaporated under reduced pressure and GQD powder was dried with acetone. The yields of p-GQDs and GQD-1700 were 77.51 and 68.75mg, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on both as-received and modified graphite electrodes using a Rigaku SmartLab diffractometer operating with Cu Kα1,2 radiation at 30mA and 40kV, in the 2θ range from 10 to 80° (using scan rate of 7°/min and 0.02°/step).

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was performed on a TEM Philips CM200 operated at 200kV. Samples were prepared by drop casting of GQD water dispersions on carbon coated copper grid with 300 meshes.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) measurements were carried out using Quesant microscope operating in tapping mode in air, at room temperature. We used AFM Q-WM300 probe, rotated, monolithic silicon probe for non-contact high frequency applications. AFM tip has a radius less than 10nm, height of 17μm, set back of 15μm, half cone angle of 20−25° along cantilever axis 25–30° from side 10° at the apex. The accuracy of the AFM diameter determination is improved by tip deconvolution. Considering the diameter of AFM tip and the larger dimensions of particles, we calculated real particle diameter from Eq. (1):
(1) rc =r (cos θ0 +(cos2 θ0 +(1+sin θ0)(−1+(tan θ0/cos θ0)+tan2 θ0))1/2),
where r is the particle radius, θ0 is the mean half angle of the tip and rc is the AFM radius of a particle [31]. Water dispersions of GQD were deposited on mica substrate by spin coating and imaged after drying. Diameter distribution analysis for both p-GQD and GQD-1700 is calculated from around 200 quantum dots in each sample.

X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed with a VG Microtech ESCA 3000 Multilab, equipped with a dual Mg/Al anode. The sample powders were analyzed mounted on a double-sided adhesive tape. The samples were excited by the unmonochromatized Al Kα source (1486.6eV) run at 14kV and 15mA. The analyser was operated in constant analyser energy (CAE) mode. For the individual peak energy regions, a pass energy of 20eV set across the hemispheres was used. Survey spectra were measured at 50eV pass energy. The pressure in the analysis chamber was in the range of 10−8 Torr during data collection. The binding energy values are quoted with a precision of ±0.15eV and the atomic percentage with a precision of ±10%.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode on Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FTIR instrument. The spectral resolution was 2cm−1. GQD powders were used for the measurements.

Raman spectroscopy measurements of graphite electrodes and graphene quantum dots deposited on Аg/glass support were obtained from a Thermo Scientific DXR microscope using 532nm excitation line from a diode pumped, solid state laser. Laser power was 2mW. The spot size of laser beam was 0.7μm. The spectral resolution was 1cm−1. Acquisition time was 100s (10×10s) for all spectra. Ten Raman spectra of each sample were recorded, at different areas of the sample. The ratios between D and G bands were calculated from their intensities in normalized spectra.

UV–vis spectra of the GQD solutions (concentration of 1mg/ml) were prepared with MilliQ water and these solutions were used for the fluorescence measurements, in the wavelength range of 260–510nm at 20°C using an Avantes UV–vis spectrophotometer (Apeldoorn, The Netherlands). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Evolution 600 UV–vis spectrophotometer, equipped with DRA-EV-600 Diffuse Reflectance Accessory. The diffuse reflectance was obtained over the range of 300 to 800nm at 20°C, in a powder samples.

Photoluminescence spectra were recorded on the Fluorolog-3 Model FL 3–221 spectrofluorometer system (HORIBA Jobin–Yvon). Emission spectra were measured utilizing a 450W Xenon lamp as an excitation source. Integration times were 0.2s. Emission spectra for study of excitation depend behavior of GQDs were obtained by a Lambda LS-55 Perkin Elmer instrument. The lifetime was measured using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) option of the Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3spectrofluorometer. The sample was excited by a pulsed NanoLED at 328nm (pulse duration <1ns; fwhm=14nm).

Quantum yield was measured according to established procedure [32]. Quinine sulphate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 360nm) was chose as a standard. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbencies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength (360nm). Excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5.0nm during PL spectra recording.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out at room temperature by using an Autolab 302N potentiostat/galvanostat and Autolab RDE, both equipped with a three-electrode cell under a stream of purified N2. The measurements of all potentials took place with respect to the Ag/AgCl electrode, separated from the solution by a Vycor frit and located close to the tip of the working electrode to minimize the ohmic drop. The counter electrode was a Pt ring directly dipped in the solution. The supporting electrolyte was composed of 0.10M phosphate buffer solution (pH of 7.40). All presented results are averages of three measurements.

Electrochemical cutting of graphene is a general procedure, which appeared in the last few years for the production of GQDs. The synthetic procedure is based on the employ of graphite rods in electrolytic solution. When current flow is established, different electrolytic species are formed and they cause the oxidation of graphitic rods [30]. In raw form, graphite/graphitic rods contain both graphite and amorphous carbon. It is already known that thermal annealing of graphite in vacuum causes an increase in the crystallinity and grain size [33].

Starting from this experimental evidence, we have decided to anneal the graphite rods before the electrochemical production of GQDs takes place. In our hypothesis, the annealing process may give us a purer starting material which may offer GQDs with improved features such as enhanced optical properties due to bigger grains of the starting material.

Therefore, we annealed the graphite rods at 1700°C in vacuum for 3h. Subsequently, XRD measurements were employed for the characterization of as-received and annealed rods (Fig. 1 a). XRD pattern of as-received graphite electrode shows a strong peak at 26.56°(d-spacing of 0.336nm). This peak corresponds to interlayer distance between graphene sheets. After annealing of the graphite rods, the strong peak stayed at same position (at 26.56°). The crystallite size was calculated using Halder-Wagner method [34]. For as-received graphite electrode, an average crystallite size is 20nm and for the annealed electrode this parameter is 25nm. XRD analysis shows that the thermal annealing causes the increase in crystallite size but it has no effect on d-spacing of graphite.

The changes in structural order of graphite electrodes were investigated by Raman spectroscopy. In Fig. 1b, Raman spectra of both as-received and annealed electrodes show D-, G- and 2D-bands. Although the positions of all three bands are remained the same after the annealing process (D-band at 1347, G- at 1580 and 2D-band at 2700cm−1), the intensity ratio between D- and G-bands (ID/IG) is changed. The annealing caused the lowering of the ID/IG from 0.44 to 0.27 which indicates the improvement in the structural order of graphite electrodes. Bots et al. analyzed structural changes of graphene oxide exposed to 127 to 2400°C and showed at temperature higher than 600°C, the reduction of graphene occurred with the conversion of hybridized C atoms from sp3 to sp2, while at 1000°C the reduction was even more efficient leading to the formation of graphene with less than 2% of O atoms and with 81.5% of sp2C [35]. At temperatures between 2000 and 2400°C, O free graphite with increased sp2 domains (up to 86% of sp2C) was obtained. Huh showed that in the range 1000–2000°C, a bottom-up layer stacking of graphene as a crystal growth was occurred with a decrease in the number of defects while at temperatures 1600, 1800 and 2000°C, defects were the lowest and mostly from sp3C atoms [36]. Considering these and our results, remained defects in our graphite stems mostly from sp3C, while the main reason for the lowering of ID/IG values and the increase in crystallite size is consequence of conversion of C atoms from sp3 to sp2 and increased sp2 domains.

After annealing of electrodes, electrochemical cutting was performed for the production of GQDs, and the material isolated from the solution was characterized. Firstly the surface morphology of GQDs was investigated by means of atomic force and transmission electron microscopy. TEM and AFM images (Fig. 2 ) show that, in both p-GQD and GQD-1700 samples, quantum dots are well dispersed with uniform morphology. TEM micrographs show that both GQD samples have sphere-like shape (Fig. 2(a and b)). The thicknesses of p-GQDs (Fig. 2d) are largely in the range between 1 and 2nm, with an average thickness of 1.17nm. This value corresponds to an average layer number of ∼3 [37]. For GQDs-1700, the thicknesses are dominantly below 1nm with an average value of 0.87nm (Fig. 2g). Therefore, quantum dots in GQD-1700 sample consist of two graphene layers. Histograms of the lateral size distribution show that the most abundant fraction of p-GQDs are 25nm (Fig. 2e) while GQDs-1700 have a lateral size of around 60 nm-Fig. 2h.

The real diameter of the most abundant fraction of p-GQDs is 20.7nm, calculated from tip deconvolution. As for GQDs-1700, the real diameter is 49.8nm. These results show that annealing procedure increases the size of the GQDs by a factor of 2 and reduces the number of graphene layers from 3 to 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural properties and photoluminescence of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The next step is their chemical analysis by means of UV–vis, XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy followed by analysis of their photoluminescence.

GQDs have interesting properties which can be exploited in the field of photoelectric applications. For these applications, the energy gaps and the fluorescence properties are very important and can be modified by changing the size, shape and the functional groups of GQDs [38,39]. UV–vis spectra of p-GQD and GQD-1700 samples are presented in Fig. 3 a. GQDs typically show strong optical absorption in the UV region. A shoulder of absorption peak at 267nm can be noticed for p-GQDs and this band is assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [40,41]. As for GQD-1700 sample, the absorption peak is broader and blue shifted to around 240nm. Moreover, in the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs-1700 we detected a very broad peak at 315nm which corresponds to n-π* transition peak of CO bond (inset of Fig. 3a) [8]. Our UV–vis results confirmed previous reports describing that position of the absorption peak depends on the preparation method as well as the oxygen content [42,43]. These results indicated the higher oxidation in GQDs-1700. The differences in the oxygen content of p-GQDs and GQDs-1700 are closer investigated with XPS.

In Fig. 3b, the survey XPS spectra show strong peaks from C 1s and O 1s, as well as peaks from impurities: Na+ and Cl−. Deconvoluted high-resolution C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of p-GQDs are presented in Fig. 3(c and e) as well as for GQD-1700 sample in Fig. 3(d and f).

In Table 1 , corresponding XPS data of p-GQD and GQD-1700 samples show a dominant graphitic C1s peak at 284.2eV, peak at 286.4eV which stems from sp3 carbon and CO peak at 288.2eV [44]. As for GQD-1700 sample, contribution of sp2 domain increased while sp3 domain disappeared completely from dot structures. The O/C atomic ratio for p-GQDs is 0.58 and for GQDs-1700 is 0.64. The increased O/C atomic ratio suggests a higher oxidation for GQDs-1700.

Fig. 4 a presents FT-IR spectra of p-GQDs (curve 1) and modified GQDs-1700 (curve 2). FT-IR spectra of both samples show peaks near 1570 and 1400cm−1 associated with the stretching of CC bonds, which indicates the presence of sp2 hybridized graphitic cores [45]. The peak at 1052cm−1 stems from CO stretching vibrations of alkoxy or epoxy functional groups. The emerging peaks at 1011cm−1 and 1365cm−1 are related to vibration of CO bonds and symmetric stretching of carboxyl groups, respectively. The band at around 1700cm−1 indicates the existence of carbonyl (CO) groups. Peaks at 2700 and 2760cm−1 stem from CH vibrations of aromatic aldehyde. Two peaks at 2870 and 2930cm−1 are associated with stretching vibrations of CH and CH2 bonds. The broad band at 3400cm−1 is associated with vibrations of OH bonds from physically absorbed water, which is a consequence of good hydrophilic properties of GQDs.

FT-IR and XPS results indicate the presence of CC, CO and OH bonds in both p-GQDs and GQDs-1700 which are the strong evidence of functionalization. Furthermore, on the basis of XPS measurements it can be concluded that both GQD samples are composed of graphitic domains and CO groups. XPS analysis showed that CO bond are not present in structure of GQDs-1700.

In Fig. 4b, Raman spectra of p-GQDs and GQDs-1700 are presented. The most prominent features in the Raman spectra of both samples are the so-called G-band appearing at 1580cm−1 and the D-band at around 1350cm−1. The value of ID/IG ratio represents a measure of disorder in graphene structure and for p-GQDs is 0.91, while for GQDs-1700 this ratio is 0.94.

For investigation of the optical band gap, diffusion spectroscopy is used. The remission function of Kubelka-Munk was applied to calculate the band gap values: F(R)=(1−R)2/2R [46]. For p-GQDs, the optical band gap is 1.99eV while for GQDs-1700 it is 1.97eV. The obtained values of optical band gap are almost 2 times smaller than those obtained for GQDs prepared by hydrothermal method (lateral sizes of GQDs prepared electrochemically were 1.5–3 times larger than those prepared by hydrothermal method). These results are in agreement with the assumption that the band gap depends on the size of the graphene fragments and generally decreases with an increase in the size [47–49]. The value of the band gap strongly affects PL emission, which will be analyzed in the further text.

GQDs prepared by different methods can show different photoluminescence, from deep ultraviolet to blue, green, yellow and red light emission [16,18,50,51]. Fluorophores in the structure of GQDs are actually aromatic π-conjugated sp2 hybridized C atoms which create subsystems surrounded by sp3 hybridized C atoms [52]. Their photoluminescence (PL) arises from intrinsic state emission or defect state emission [53]. The intrinsic state emission is induced by quantum size effect, zigzag edge sites or recombination of localized electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, defect state emission arises from defect effect (energy traps) [3,54]. Fig. 5 a (curves 1 and 2) shows PL spectra of p-GQDs and GQDs-1700, respectively. Both p- and GQDs-1700 emit blue light (centered at 460 and 486nm, respectively) under the excitation of 328nm. For GQDs-1700, PL emission is red shifted for 26nm and its intensity is four times higher compared to p-GQDs.

Blue photoluminescence of GQDs is a result of quantum confinement of electrons inside the π-conjugated C domains and zigzag edge effect [55]. Actually, in the structure of GQDs quantum confinement effect reflects in the size of graphene sp2 domains rather than actual particle size. As XPS analysis showed, GQDs-1700 contain the larger percentage of sp2C atoms and thus larger π-conjugated domains compared to p-GQDs.

On the other side, free zigzag sites can also contribute to blue photoluminescence of GQDs [56]. By changing the pH from 1 to 13, we investigated PL emission of both samples (Fig. 6 ). At pH 1, free zigzag edges are protonated which leads to PL quenching as a result of breaking the emissive triplet carbene state [2]. For both p-GQDs and GQDs-1700, PL emission at pH 1 is reduced, but this effect is more prominent for p-GQDs. At pH 13, the intensity of PL emission was restored for p-GQDs but for GQD-1700 the PL intensity was lower than at pH 7.

The red shift of PL emission in a case of GQDs-1700 can be results of increase in the size of aromatic π-conjugated domains in GQDs, and consequently decrease of their band gap. But also same shift can be caused by surface defects: oxygen functional groups decorating the intrinsic sp2 domains at the edges and/or on the basal planes [51,57,58]. As FTIR and XPS analyses showed, both types of synthesized GQDs have oxygen-containing functional groups. The presence of the isolated sp2 clusters within the sp3 C–O matrix leads to the localization of electron-hole pairs, facilitating radiative recombination of small clusters [54,59,60]. These functional groups can also lead to the formation of “surface states” on GQDs, with energy levels between π and π* states of CC bonds, and series of emissive traps. Thereafter, the radiation from recombination of trapped excitons leads to the variation and red shift of emission. Thus, oxidized carbon results in localized electronic states and the emission is predominantly from the electron transitions among/between the non-oxidized and oxidized carbon region. Hence the red shift of PL of GQDs-1700 can be assign to higher degree of surface oxidation.

Both GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior, as can be seen in Fig. 5(c and d). That is a common phenomenon reported in GQDs and it was observed for other GQDs prepared by electrochemical method, explained in terms of emissive traps, electronic conjugate structures and free zigzag sites. Based on the FT-IR and XPS results, different kinds of functional groups are presented in the structure of these GQDs. The functional groups have various energy levels, which may result in a series of emissive traps. Different surface-state emissive traps emerge with various excitation wavelengths.

Also, different widths of emission profiles can be observed in Fig. 5a. The wider emission profiles of GQDs-1700 than for p-GQDs is a consequence of the wider size distribution of quantum dots in GQD-1700 sample as well as differences in the oxidation degree.

The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of p-GQDs and GQDs-1700 was estimated to be 0.7 and 1.8%, respectively. As reference, the molecule quinine sulphate (λexc =360nm) was used. Compared to other results [61], the lower QY of our GQDs may be ascribed to the different experimental method and sample states.

In Fig. 5b, the PL decay curves of p-GQDs (curve 1) and GQDs-1700 (curve 2) are presented. Both decay curves can be well fitted using a bi-exponential function of I(t)=A1exp(−t/τ1)+ A2exp(−t/τ2). Here, A1, A2, τ1 and τ2 are the percentage contribution and value of the faster (slower) lifetime component, respectively, from which we can define a weighted-average lifetime of τaverage =A1τ1 +A2τ2 [62]. Based on the measured values, average lifetime of p-GQDs is τaverage =0.45ns under excitation of 328nm, while average lifetime of GQDs-1700 is 2.95ns under the same excitation. The average lifetime of GQDs-1700 is almost six times longer than for p-GQDs. Compared to the results of other authors, sample of GQD-1700 has 3 times longer average lifetime [54].

Above presented results showed that: (a) the real size of GQD-1700 was increased resulting in the lager π domains which control the quantum size effect; (b) zigzag edges significantly contribute to PL of p-GQDs while they are less present in GQDs-1700; (c) higher amount of CO in GQDs-1700 resulting in a greater number of carbonyl-based green PL centers [63]; (d) increased O-containing groups in GQD-1700 caused formation of “surface states”, with energy levels between π and π* states.

Apart from optical and structural properties, charge storage behavior of both samples was studied. In Fig. 7 (a and b) cyclic voltammograms of p-GQDs and GQDs-1700 measured in a potential range of −1.0V to 1.0V at scan rate of 25mVs−1 are shown. The shape of CV curves for both samples was maintained even after 7 cycles which indicates a high electrochemical stability and stable capacitance behavior of analyzed GQDs.

Both CV curves present the same rectangular shape and they have similar oxidation states. On the other hand, the qualitative differences appeared in the cathodic reduction part. The reduction peak, which is appeared at −0.5V for the p-GQDs, is shifted in a more negative potential in the case of GQDs-1700 (−0.75V). In addition, for the latter is appeared a second reduction process and it is at −0.1V. This peak is due to the greater number of carbonyl groups which are contained in the GQDs-1700 [64,65].

The capacitive behavior of quantum dots is evaluated using same 3-electrode configuration in aqueous system at pH of 7.4. Dependence of CV curves with scan rates, at 25, 36, 53, 77, 113, 165, 352, 514 and 750mVs−1 is presented in Fig. 7(c and d). CV curves for both samples do not show very rectangular shape suggesting pseudo-capacitance behavior. Considering that the shape of CV curves are preserved even up to 750mVs−1, stabile and reproducible capacitance behavior has been further proved. The current response increased with scan rates. As can be observed in Fig. 7(b and c), at all currents each CV curve has similar rectangular shape which indicates similar oxidation states.

In Fig. 8 a, it can be clearly observed that the current intensity of the GQDs-1700 is lower than of the p-GQDs. This variation can due to the differences in the size, defect sites or in the amount of the functional groups (GQDs-1700 are more oxidized).

For GQD samples charge/discharge capacitances were calculated by integration of the area under corresponding anodic and cathodic parts of CV curves. The obtained values (calculated using the areal loading of electrodes) with respect to the scan rate are showed in Fig. 8b. As we already noticed in the previous figure, the higher current intensities and consequently capacitances is measured for p-GQDs.

Some studies showed that with increasing the number active edges and functional groups based on oxygen leads to high specific capacitance as results of better wettability and larger surface area [66,67]. Here, on the opposite increased number of oxygen containing functional groups combined with higher diameter cased the lowering of the capacitance. Considering more oxygen functional groups, π conjugated structure in GQDs can be disrupted and created defects which are probably responsible for lower capacitance of GQDs-1700. Additional, not all oxygen functional groups contribute in the same way [67]. Considering that cathodic peak represent electrochemical reduction of oxygen containing functional groups, such as peroxyl, aldehyde and epoxy groups, the apparent amount of these reducible groups in GQDs can be calculated using Faraday’s laws of electrolysis [27]. Applying the Eq. (2):
(2) N(μmol/g)=Q(μC)/(zF(C/mol)W(g)),
the apparent amount of reducible groups is calculated, where Q (in μC) is obtained by integration of corresponding cathodic peak, z is the number of electrons involved in reduction of each group (2 in this case), F is Faraday constant (96.48C/mol) and W is mass of samples deposited on working electrode in g. At scan rate of 50mV/s, the values of apparent reducible groups in p-GQDs and GQDs-1700 are 184.53 and 80.20μmol/g.

These analyses lead us to conclusion that charge/discharge properties of GQDs are strongly affected by the type and amount of oxygen containing functional groups: a higher content of peroxyl, aldehyde and epoxy groups resulted in better capacitance behavior while the increase in keto with reduced number of above mentioned groups caused a lower capacitance.

On the basis of the results presented above, a mechanism of GQDs-1700 formation is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 9 . The thermal annealing of graphite electrodes causes the increase in crystallite size and structural order. It was observed that the crystallinity and structural disorder of graphite used for oxidation had significant effects on the resulting oxidized structure [68,69]. We also observed that carbon nanotubes undergo oxidative cutting upon gamma irradiation at defect sites where the bonds are the most fragile [70]. The process of oxidation graphene like material usually starts at defect sites, such as topological defects or edges, and further progress from there. In the case of increased crystallinity and less defects, the grains are bigger and oxidation causes formation of O- functional groups on their basal planes as well as carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups at edges. In the course of electrochemical oxidation, oxygen and hydroxyl radicals attack graphite electrodes and cause the releasing of GQDs in solution [71,72]. Raman analysis showed that more defective structure is noticed for GQD-1700 sample. On the contrary, AFM and TEM indicate increased size of GQDs-1700, and thus they possess less edge defects in overall quantum dot structure. As XPS analysis showed that GQDs-1700 contained higher amount of oxygen in their structure. These results suggest that radicals attack graphite electrodes not only at grain edges, as proposed for p-GQDs, but also inside of electrode grains. Considering all these results, we suggest that GQDs-1700 contain higher amount of oxygen containing groups bonded to edges of defects inside of graphene basal plane (Fig. 9). The higher luminescence of GQDs-1700 compared to p-GQDs is most likely result of increase in defect sites.

A new approach for the increase of photoluminescence of GQDs prepared by using the electrochemical method was presented. In the case of p-GQDs we used as-received spectroscopic graphite rods as electrodes, while for GQDs-1700 as precursors were used graphitic rods modified by annealing at 1700°C in vacuum. The annealing of the rod is yielding to materials which are bigger in size and more oxidized. These structural changes are the main factor for the appearance of higher luminescence and the double relative QY for the GQDs-1700. In addition they show differences in the electrochemical behavior which appeared as a decrease in the capacitance. The proposed modified electrochemical approach for GQD synthesis appears to be a considerable method to manipulate the synthesis of GQDs with different sizes and different optical properties. Future work will be focused in the more advanced photoelectrochemical characterization for applying these materials in different devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Prof. Maurizio Prato for valuable comments and discussion, Prof. Ivanka Holclajtner-Antunović for Raman measurements and Dr. Nadica Abazović for diffusive reflectance measurements. This research was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Republic of Serbia (project no. 172003) and project of bilateral collaboration between Serbia and Slovakia, 2017–2018 (project no. SK-SRB-2016-0038). Also, this work was supported by the SASPRO Programme, project no. 1237/02/02-b. The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union’s Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427. Research has been further co-funded by the Slovak Academy of Sciences and by the VEGA project (2/0093/16).
